answer:
S(xy) :- V(x), N(y).
S(xyz) :- V(x), N(y), S(z).
V(help).
V(let).
N(John).
N(Kim).
reasoning: The underlined English strings are sequences of one or more verb–name pairs like (help|let)(John|Kim). The first rule builds a single pair; the second rule recurses to add more pairs, generating all well-formed combinations.

answer:
R(wy, xz) :- T(w, x), R(y, z).
R(w, x) :- T(w, x).
S(xy) :- R(x, y).
T(em Hans, hälfe).
T(em Jan, hälfe).
T(de Hans, laa).
T(de Jan, laa).
T(em Hans, hälfed).
T(em Jan, hälfed).
T(de Hans, lönd).
T(de Jan, lönd).
reasoning: Swiss German shows cross-serial dependencies: each NP (case-marked) pairs with its selecting verb. T encodes these NP–verb pairs. R concatenates any number of such pairs while preserving their order on both sides; S then flattens the pair into a single string, yielding the observed NP block followed by the verb block and allowing mixes of hälfe/ laa (and their finite forms).
